title: Psalms 144
subtitle: Praise Be to the LORD My Rock

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “What Are Human Beings That You Care for Them?”

heb.paragraph: "They are like a breath (hebel); their days are like a fleeting shadow" (v.4). David’s battle psalm. God is his rock, fortress, stronghold, deliverer, shield, refuge (vv.1–2). Then the Psalm-8 question: "What are human beings that you care for them?" (v.3). The answer: we are hebel and shadow — but God trains our hands for battle (v.1) and parts the heavens to rescue (v.5).

notes.note: "Praise be to the LORD my Rock, who trains my hands for war. He is my loving God and my fortress, my stronghold and my deliverer, my shield, in whom I take refuge." Six military-refuge titles in two verses.
"LORD, what are human beings that you care for them? They are like a breath; their days are like a fleeting shadow." The Psalm 8 question with Ecclesiastes’ answer: we are hebel. Yet God cares.

"Part your heavens, LORD, and come down; touch the mountains so that they smoke. Send forth lightning and scatter the enemy." Theophany-request: come down, smoke mountains, scatter with lightning. The prayer for cosmic intervention.

sections.header: Verses 9–15 — “Blessed Is the People Whose God Is the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Blessed (ʾashre) is the people whose God is the LORD" (v.15). David sings a new song on the ten-stringed lyre (v.9). After the battle: prosperous sons and daughters, overflowing barns, abundant flocks, strong oxen, no breach in walls, no cry of distress in the streets (vv.12–14). The vision of shalom: not just the absence of war but the presence of abundance. The final beatitude: blessed is the people whose God is the LORD.

notes.note: "Our sons in their youth like well-nurtured plants; our daughters like pillars carved to adorn a palace. Our barns full, our flocks numbering thousands. No breach, no captivity, no cry of distress." The shalom vision: every dimension of life — family, agriculture, security — flourishing.
"Blessed is the people whose God is the LORD." The psalm’s closing beatitude. National blessedness defined by one relationship: whose God is the LORD.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: He is my loving God and my fortress, my stronghold and my deliverer, my shield, in whom I take refuge…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVwhose mouths are full of lies, whose right hands are deceitful.ESVwhose mouths speak lies and whose right hand is a right hand of falsehood.NIVHe is my loving God and my fortress, my stronghold and my deliverer, my shield, in whom I take refuge, who subdues peoples under me.ESVhe is my steadfast love and my fortress, my stronghold and my deliverer, my shield and he in whom I take refuge, who subdues peoples under me.

rows.label: Hebel and Theophany — 144:1–8
rows.text: Rock, fortress, shield. "We are breath." "Part your heavens and come down!"

hebtext.note: "They are like a breath (hebel); their days are like a fleeting shadow" (v.4). David’s battle psalm. God is his rock, fortress, stronghold, deliverer, shield, refuge (vv.1–2). Then the Psalm-8 question: "What are human beings that you care for them?" (v.3). The answer: we are hebel and shadow — but God trains our hands for battle (v.1) and parts the heavens to rescue (v.5).

"Blessed (ʾashre) is the people whose God is the LORD" (v.15). David sings a new song on the ten-stringed lyre (v.9). After the battle: prosperous sons and daughters, overflowing barns, abundant flocks, strong oxen, no breach in walls, no cry of distress in the streets (vv.12–14). The vision of shalom: not just the absence of war but the presence of abundance. The final beatitude: blessed is the people whose God is the LORD.

themes.note: Covenant: national blessedness through divine relationship. Sovereignty: theophany-request + shalom vision. The Psalter begins to ascend toward its finale.
